message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ethnic inequalities in compulsory psychiatric hospital detentions are well-documented in the UK and internationally. It is unknown how UK COVID-19 ‘lockdown measures’, which led to restrictions in public movement, gatherings, in-person health service delivery, and changes to police powers, further impacted inequalities.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aims</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this study, we assessed whether national ‘lockdown’ measures impacted ethnic inequalities in voluntary and compulsory psychiatric hospital admissions, during the COVID-19 pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Daily counts of admissions and detentions to psychiatric hospitals were extracted from a large population-level sample of secondary mental health service users in southeast London. Changes during two COVID-19 lockdown periods, over 2020-2021, were compared with pre-pandemic periods (2016-2019) with the use of a regression discontinuity in time design to assess ethnic inequalities in voluntary/ compulsory mental health admissions.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Compared to the pre-pandemic reference (2016-2019), after adjusting for seasonal and weekly trends, overall admissions to mental health units dropped during the first COVID-19 lockdown (Incidence Rate Ratio (IRR) 0.87 (95% CI: 0.75-1.00) but with more compulsory detentions (1.25 (1.05-1.54)). This was mostly due to higher compulsory detentions in the Black Caribbean group (1.54 (1.08-2.19)). During the second COVID-19 lockdown, whereas total daily admissions remained similar to the pre-pandemic reference (1.03 (0.92-1.15)), total new daily detentions was elevated (1.28 (1.11-1.49)), specifically in Black Caribbean (1.53 (1.14, 2.06)) and Black African (1.57 (1.06-2.34)) groups.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 lockdown measures exacerbated pre-existing ethnic inequalities in compulsory psychiatric detention, particularly for those from Black Caribbean and Black African backgrounds. There is a need to address ethnic inequalities in compulsory psychiatric detentions and attend to exacerbations of pre-existing inequalities during health emergencies like the COVID-19 pandemic. This cannot be achieved without addressing systemic racism within criminal justice and healthcare systems and tackling inequalities in wider social and economic determinants of mental health.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Health Foundation, NIHR, UKRI.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Ethnic inequalities in compulsory psychiatric hospital detentions during UK COVID-19 ‘lockdowns’: A Regression Discontinuity Design in time study
